turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the court this morning in Case 23-1239, Barnes v. Felix.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: This case arises from a tragic traffic stop. A law enforcement officer pulled over a driver on a highway because of outstanding toll violations on the driver's car. After about two minutes of talk, the officer ordered the driver, named Ashtian Barnes, out of his car. Barnes opened the door, but did not get out; instead, he turned the ignition back on, and as the car began to move forward, the officer jumped on its doorsill. Within two seconds lacking any visibility into the car, he fired two quick shots inside. Barnes was hit but managed to stop the car. By the time backup arrived, Barnes was dead. Barnes' mother sued the officer and his department on her son's behalf. She claimed that the officer had used excessive force against her son, which if true would have violated his Fourth Amendment rights. In a suit of that kind, this court has long held. The question is whether the officer's conduct was objectively reasonable? And we have held that that question is answered by looking at the totality of the circumstances by all the facts, circumstances, and context relating to the incident. The question in this case is whether the courts hearing Barnes' lawsuit in the Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit were faithful to those principles. Those courts applied something called the moment of threat rule when they evaluated Barnes' claim. Under that rule, a court can look only at the situation existing at the exact moment of the threat that sparked the fatal shooting. That meant here that the courts looked only to the two-second period after the officer jumped on the doorsill and before he fired his first shot. And with their eyes trained only on those two last seconds, the courts had little difficulty finding the officer's shooting reasonable. At that point, they explained he was hanging on to a moving vehicle and could reasonably believe his life was in danger unless the car was stopped. But that narrow focus we hold today, that focus on only the final seconds of an encounter is inconsistent with our rule that courts look to the totality of the circumstances. The situation at the moment of the shooting is of course quite important, but sometimes context matters. Earlier events might bear on how a reasonable officer would have understood and responded to later ones. And the courts below were wrong to exclude that possibility, to look only at what was happening when the officer pulled the trigger and not at all the events that had led up to that time. Because some or all of those preceding events might be relevant in evaluating the reasonableness of the eventual shooting. The totality of the circumstances inquiry, as we have explained it, has no time limit. A court performing that inquiry cannot put on chronological blinders. Having said that much, we return this case to the courts in the Fifth Circuit to do the proper totality of the circumstances analysis. The opinion of the court is unanimous. Justice Kavanaugh has filed a concurring opinion in which Justices Thomas, Alito and Barrett have joined.
